I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. He's never really written anything before. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Strong storytelling foundation** - The essay has a clear beginning, middle, and end with good narrative flow. The specific details (Marcus, "pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way") make it feel real and engaging rather than generic.

**Authentic voice** - This sounds like an actual person telling their story, not someone trying to sound "essay-like." That's harder than it seems and many students struggle with it.

**Genuine reflection** - The insights feel earned, not forced. The line about "you don't know what you don't know" and the final line about failure being "just information" show real thinking. The honesty about the mean comment still stinging demonstrates maturity.

**Good pivot** - The debate team connection works and shows growth beyond just "I felt bad then got over it."

## What Needs Work:

**Sentence variety** - Many short, choppy sentences in a row. Try combining some: "The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly..." could become "During the three-day tryout, I quickly realized..."

**Show more, tell less** - "I was really upset for a while" could be a moment with actual scenes/details instead of summary.

**Develop the middle** - The scrimmage scene is the emotional core but feels rushed. This is where we should really *feel* the failure happening.

**Tone consistency** - "So yeah" is too casual for the otherwise thoughtful final paragraph.

## Bottom Line:
For a first essay, this is genuinely impressive. The bones are solid—keep writing.

- **Clear, authentic voice** — The writing sounds genuine and relatable. The informal tone fits a personal narrative perfectly.

# Grade: 7.5/10

## What Works:

**Authentic voice** - This sounds like a real person telling a real story. The casual tone ("So yeah," "Obviously") actually strengthens rather than weakens it. For a first-time writer, finding a natural voice is half the battle, and this student has it.

**Solid structure** - Clear beginning, middle, and end. The narrative flows logically from confidence → failure → reflection → growth.

**Vulnerability** - Admitting he lied to his mom, sitting with the sting of that "stay out of the way" comment - these honest moments give the essay emotional weight.

## What Could Be Stronger:

**Show more, tell less** - Phrases like "I was really upset for a while" are vague. What did that look like? What did he do? A scene or two with more sensory detail would add punch.

**Sentence variety** - Many sentences follow the same simple structure. Mixing it up would improve the flow.

**The debate team pivot** - This is mentioned but not developed. One specific example from debate would help us see the contrast.

**Opening** - "The first time I really failed..." is functional but generic. Starting with a specific moment might hook readers better.

## Bottom Line:
For someone who's never written before, this is impressive work. It's honest, well-organized, and thoughtful. The writing is clear and the insights feel earned, not manufactured. Strong foundation to build on.
